agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly conflicting, non-negotiable requirements about whether courier GPS location should be visible to customers.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver who picks up and delivers packages to customers"},{"term":"Real-time location","definition":"The courier's current GPS coordinates, updated continuously during an active delivery"},{"term":"Active delivery","definition":"The period from when a courier picks up a package to when it is delivered to the customer"},{"term":"Customer tracking","definition":"The ability for a package recipient to observe delivery progress, potentially including courier location"},{"term":"Courier privacy","definition":"The protection of a courier's precise movements and location data from external visibility"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"During an active delivery, delivery progress information of some form must be available to the customer.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Both teams agree customers need delivery visibility — the dispute is over the granularity and form of that visibility. A VP could define the exact level.","testable_assertion":"When a delivery is in progress, the customer can access a tracking view that shows delivery status information."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Courier location data must be collected during active deliveries for operational purposes (routing, dispatch, ETA calculation).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Regardless of whether location is shown to customers, the business needs GPS data for logistics operations. Without it, dispatch and ETA systems cannot function.","testable_assertion":"The system records courier GPS coordinates at regular intervals during an active delivery, and these are available to internal operations systems."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Courier personally identifiable location data must not be stored or displayed outside of an active delivery window.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Showing courier location when no delivery is active would be surveillance with no business justification and likely violates labor and privacy regulations.","testable_assertion":"No courier GPS data is exposed to any customer-facing system when the courier has no active delivery to that customer."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The level of location detail shown to customers must balance transparency with courier privacy and retention.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is the core policy decision that must be resolved by leadership. The exact balance point is a business policy, not a system invariant.","testable_assertion":"The customer tracking view displays location information at a granularity level approved by both Customer Success and Operations stakeholders."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A customer has an active delivery in progress","when":"The customer opens the tracking view","then":"The system displays delivery progress information (the form and granularity of which is pending stakeholder resolution)"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A courier is on an active delivery","when":"The system collects the courier's GPS coordinates","then":"The coordinates are available to internal dispatch and ETA calculation systems"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"A courier has no active delivery to a specific customer","when":"That customer attempts to view courier location","then":"No courier location data of any kind is displayed"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A delivery is in progress","when":"The system calculates an ETA for the customer","then":"The ETA is derived from real GPS data and is accurate to within a reasonable tolerance"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier completes delivery but GPS stream has not yet terminated","expected_behavior":"Customer tracking view must immediately stop showing any location data upon delivery confirmation, regardless of GPS stream state"},{"scenario":"Courier is between stops on a multi-drop route","expected_behavior":"Only the next customer in the route should see any tracking data; previous and future customers should see no courier location"},{"scenario":"GPS signal is lost or inaccurate during delivery","expected_behavior":"System should show last known status with a staleness indicator rather than displaying stale coordinates as current"},{"scenario":"Customer screenshots or records courier location data","expected_behavior":"Even if location is shown, the system should minimize the precision and persistence of data to reduce misuse risk"},{"scenario":"Courier deviates significantly from expected route","expected_behavior":"Customer should see updated ETA but not necessarily the deviation path, to avoid exposing courier personal stops or operational rerouting"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Unresolved, this conflict blocks feature delivery entirely. If resolved poorly toward full transparency, driver attrition increases recruitment and training costs (typically $3K-$5K per driver). If resolved poorly toward full opacity, customer satisfaction and competitive differentiation suffer, risking churn.","operational":"Driver retention is an existential operational concern — courier marketplaces are highly competitive and losing drivers degrades delivery capacity directly. Showing exact GPS also creates liability exposure if location data is breached or misused.","user":"Customers expect some form of delivery visibility — it is table stakes in 2026. However, research consistently shows customers primarily want accurate ETAs and status updates, not raw GPS coordinates. The exact coordinates are a proxy for what customers actually need: confidence their package is on its way.","risk":"High risk on multiple fronts: (1) Privacy/labor law risk if courier location is exposed without consent frameworks, (2) Driver retention risk if surveillance perception persists, (3) Competitive risk if no tracking is offered at all, (4) Data breach risk — courier GPS trails are sensitive PII in many jurisdictions (GDPR, CCPA).","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Delivery visibility affects trust and satisfaction. They need confidence the delivery is progressing, but not necessarily raw GPS data."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected by any location sharing. Feeling surveilled impacts job satisfaction, retention, and potentially labor relations."},{"group":"Customer Success team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics and has positioned transparency as a differentiator. Needs a solution they can market."},{"group":"Operations team","impact":"Owns driver retention and fleet capacity. Cannot afford further driver attrition from surveillance concerns."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Not yet consulted but critically affected. Courier location tracking has regulatory implications under privacy law and potentially labor law."},{"group":"Product/Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. The resolution defines the company's position on the transparency-vs-privacy spectrum."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"Two explicit, non-negotiable stakeholder requirements directly contradict each other. Until leadership arbitrates, no implementation can satisfy both. The spec is fundamentally blocked. Confidence is low because the core feature behavior — what the customer actually sees — is undefined.","high_confidence_areas":["Courier GPS data must be collected for internal operations regardless of customer visibility decision","Location data must never be shown outside an active delivery window","Customers need some form of delivery progress visibility — this is industry table stakes","The conflict is real and cannot be resolved by engineering alone"],"low_confidence_areas":["What granularity of location data (exact GPS, zone, ETA-only) will be shown to customers","Whether a consent framework for couriers could unlock partial location sharing","Whether legal/regulatory constraints further restrict options beyond what either team has stated","Whether Customer Success's 'transparency differentiator' specifically requires GPS or could be satisfied by superior ETA accuracy"],"assumptions":["Both teams have legitimate authority to make requirements in their respective domains","No executive decision has already been made that overrides either team","The system already collects courier GPS for operational purposes"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers as a non-negotiable transparency differentiator. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as a non-negotiable driver retention measure. These two requirements are mutually exclusive as stated — the system cannot simultaneously always show and never show courier GPS to customers.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Success","Operations","Couriers","Customers","Legal/Compliance","Product Leadership"],"options":["(A) Show exact GPS as Customer Success demands — accept driver attrition risk","(B) Show no location as Operations demands — accept competitive differentiation loss","(C) Compromise: show approximate location (zone-level or street-level, not exact GPS) with courier consent — partially satisfies both","(D) Compromise: show rich ETA and status updates derived from GPS, without exposing coordinates — reframes transparency as delivery confidence rather than surveillance","(E) Make it courier-configurable: let drivers opt in/out of location sharing, show ETA-only for opt-out drivers"],"recommendation":"Option D or E. Customer Success's actual business goal is customer confidence during delivery, not raw GPS display. Operations's actual concern is courier perception of surveillance. A solution that uses GPS data to power accurate, real-time ETAs and granular status updates (e.g., 'Courier is 3 stops away', 'Arriving in ~8 minutes') delivers transparency without exposing exact coordinates. This reframes the feature from 'watch your driver' to 'know exactly when your package arrives' — which is arguably a stronger differentiator. This requires Product Leadership to arbitrate and redefine the requirement with both teams. Engineering should NOT proceed until this is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement feature as specified by either team alone without violating the other team's stated non-negotiable requirement","business_rule_broken":"No implementable business rule can be defined when two authoritative stakeholders demand mutually exclusive behaviors for the same feature"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature is proposed to show customers their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly conflicting, non-negotiable requirements about whether courier GPS location should be visible to customers.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Customer Visibility","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver who picks up and delivers packages to customers"},{"term"

Confidence is low because the core feature behavior — what the customer actually sees — is undefined.","high_confidence_areas":["Courier GPS data must be collected for internal operations regardless of customer visibility decision","Location data must never be shown outside an active delivery window","Customers need some form of delivery progress visibility — this is industry table stakes","The conflict is real and cannot be resolved by engineering alone"],"low_confidence_areas":["What granularity of location data (exact GPS, zone, ETA-only) will be shown to customers","Whether a consent framework for couriers could unlock partial location sharing","Whether legal/regulatory constraints further restrict options beyond what either team has stated","Whether Customer Success's 'transparency differentiator' specifically requires GPS or could be satisfied by superior ETA accuracy"],"assumptions":["Both teams have legitimate authority to make requirements in their respective domains","No executive decision has already been made that overrides either team","The system already collects courier GPS for operational purposes"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires showing exact courier GPS coordinates to customers as a non-negotiable transparency differentiator. Operations requires never showing courier location to customers as a non-negotiable driver retention measure. These two requirements are mutually exclusive as stated — the system cannot simultaneously always show and never show courier GPS to customers.","affected_stakeholders":["Customer Success","Operations","Couriers","Customers","Legal/Compliance","Product Leadership"],"options":["(A) Show exact GPS as Customer Success demands — accept driver attrition risk","(B) Show no location as Operations demands — accept competitive differentiation loss","(C) Compromise: show approximate location (zone-level or street-level, not exact GPS) with courier consent — partially satisfies both","(D) Compromise: show rich ETA and status updates derived from GPS, without exposing coordinates — reframes transparency as delivery confidence rather than surveillance","(E) Make it courier-configurable: let drivers opt in/out of location sharing, show ETA-only for opt-out drivers"],"recommendation":"Option D or E. Customer Success's actual business goal is customer confidence during delivery, not raw GPS display. Operations's actual concern is courier perception of surveillance. A solution that uses GPS data to power accurate, real-time ETAs and granular status updates (e.g., 'Courier is 3 stops away', 'Arriving in ~8 minutes') delivers transparency without exposing exact coordinates. This reframes the feature from 'watch your driver' to 'know exactly when your package arrives' — which is arguably a stronger differentiator. This requires Product Leadership to arbitrate and redefine the requirement with both teams. Engineering should NOT proceed until this is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Cannot implement feature as specified by either team alone without violating the other team's stated non-negotiable requirement","business_rule_broken":"No implementable business rule can be defined when two authoritative stakeholders demand mutually exclusive behaviors for the same feature"}]}
